channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Touts ‘Fantastic Trade Deals’ With China, but Details Are Scarce
item.description: The president left Beijing following a summit with China, during which the two countries sought to stabilize their economic and political relations.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump before departing China in Beijing on Friday.

item.title: ‘For Example, Putin’: How Xi Used a Private Garden Walk to Charm Trump
item.description: Watch an exchange between the two men in which the Chinese leader sought to signal exclusivity and name-dropped Russia’s president.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo, Jiawei Wang and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: In Photos and Video: President Trump Goes to China
item.description: Mr. Trump’s trip, the first U.S. presidential visit to China in almost a decade, was closely watched for signs of whether a trade truce between the countries would hold.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Great Hall of the People (Beijing, China)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Evan Vucci
item.media:description: U.S. President Donald Trump participating in a friendship walk through Zhongnanhai Garden with Chinese leader Xi Jinping in Beijing.

item.title: A Surge of U.S. Spy Planes Over Cuba, and Retailers’ $20 Billion Bet on Physical Stores
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas and Ian Stewart
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rooftop solar panels in Havana. Cuba’s energy minister said that solar power was not a reliable alternative source for the country because its grid was too weak.

item.title: As Powell Steps Down, the Fed Confronts ‘Regime Change’
item.description: Jerome H. Powell is passing the chair’s baton to Kevin M. Warsh at the Federal Reserve, an institution that President Trump’s pick says needs an overhaul.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times, left; Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times

item.title: How Jerome Powell Managed a Chaotic Era
item.description: Our reporter Colby Smith, who covers the Federal Reserve and the economy, looks back at Jerome Powell’s tenure as chair of the Fed.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith, Nour Idriss, Stephanie Swart and Rafaela Balster

item.title: It’s Not Just U.S. Stocks. A.I. and Oil Are Moving Global Markets, Too.
item.description: Intel, along with Taiwan and South Korea, are the latest winners, our columnist says. True diversification is hard to find.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: I-Hwa Cheng/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Only a year ago, Intel needed a bailout by the U.S. government. This year, it has made a remarkable comeback.

item.title: Oil Prices Climb on Fears of Broader Energy Crunch
item.description: Hopes for an end to the war in Iran faded after President Trump failed to secure a commitment from China to help persuade Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston

item.title: A Top U.S. Commander Dismisses Reports of Civilian Deaths in Iran
item.description: Adm. Brad Cooper said a strike on an Iranian school may have been the only civilian casualty incident of the war.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Admiral Cooper told the lawmakers that his staff had been proactive in trying to prevent civilian deaths. “Our staff specifically warned the Iranian people more than 100 times about the threat of them being used as human shields,” he said.

item.title: C.I.A. Director Visits Cuba as Tensions Rise and Island Runs Out of Oil
item.description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, is the highest-ranking official in the Trump administration to visit the country.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Havana this month. In January, the Trump administration cut off oil shipments from Venezuela, which had been supplying a large amount of Cuba’s oil.

item.title: Joe Sedelmaier, Auteur Behind ‘Where’s the Beef?’ Ad, Dies at 92
item.description: He directed nearly 1,000 comedic commercials, including a much-quoted spot for Wendy’s and one for FedEx featuring a manic speed talker.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Bettmann, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Clara Peller, the octogenarian star of a classic Wendy’s commercial directed by Joe Sedelmaier, asks her famous question during a news conference in 1984.

item.title: Oysterman, Veteran, Prep-School Alum: A Senate Candidate’s Complex Class Story
item.description: Democrats are pinning their hopes of flipping the Senate on Graham Platner, who has made his working-class persona key to his campaign. His background defies easy categorization.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Sophie Park

item.title: Bernie Sanders Endorses Slate of Candidates in Bid to Cement Progressive Legacy
item.description: The five dozen endorsements mostly feature those seeking state or local office who have previously supported the Vermont senator.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Sarah Rice/Getty Images
item.media:description: Senator Bernie Sanders, an independent from Vermont, said he is working to help elect as many like-minded allies as possible up and down the ballot.

item.title: Mystery Grows Around Representative Thomas Kean Jr.’s Absence
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. last voted in Washington on March 5, citing a medical issue. An appearance planned for late May has been canceled.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Bill Clark/CQ Roll Call, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Thomas Kean Jr., a Republican congressman from New Jersey, has been out of the public eye for more than two months.

item.title: James Talarico’s Taco Order Sparks Debate in Texas Senate Race
item.description: What started as a taco stop with former President Barack Obama quickly turned into a very Texas debate over the proper breakfast taco order.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Joel Angel Juarez-Pool
item.media:description: Former President Barack Obama ordering tacos alongside Texas Senate candidate James Talarico at the restaurant Taco Joint in Austin earlier this month.

item.title: Supreme Court Allows Abortion Pill Access by Mail to Continue
item.description: A federal appeals court ruling against the Food and Drug Administration would have restricted access by mail to mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Evelyn Hockstein/Reuters
item.media:description: The clash over access to mifepristone has put the Trump administration in a difficult political position ahead of the midterm election.

item.title: Mail-Order Abortions Have Quickly Become Common
item.description: Pills shipped to states with bans are a reason abortion has increased since Roe v. Wade was overturned.
item.dc:creator: Margot Sanger-Katz and Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Amanda Andrade-Rhoades/Associated Press

item.title: Enter the Killer Robots: The Ukrainian Forging the Future of Warfare
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov, Ukraine’s 35-year-old defense minister, sees futuristic military technology as crucial to his country’s survival.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: Archaeologists Find Egyptian Mummy Buried With the ‘Iliad’
item.description: For the deceased of Roman-era Egypt, Greek literature may have offered a cheat code to a more comfortable afterlife.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
item.media:credit: Maite Mascort i Roca and Esther Pons Mellado, Mission of the University of Barcelona at Oxyrhynchus
item.media:description: The necropolis of Oxyrhynchus in Egypt, where a nonroyal mummy was found with some lines on papyrus from Book 2 of Homer’s “Iliad.”

item.title: Even Alex Murdaugh Didn’t Think His Convictions Would Be Overturned
item.description: His lawyers said he was stunned to learn that South Carolina’s top court had reversed the murder convictions. Prosecutors plan to retry the case, but a second trial could look very different.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Tracy Glantz/The State, via ZUMA Press Wire
item.media:description: Alex Murdaugh, center, during a hearing in 2024 in Columbia, S.C.

item.title: The Strange Alliance Trying to Remake American Psychiatry
item.description: The Department of Health and critical psychiatry activists are hoping to upend 40 years of medical orthodoxy.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Bergner

item.title: Deported Despite DACA: Dreamers Face Uncertainty Under Trump
item.description: The administration has said DACA isn’t a right to stay in the United States “indefinitely.” One man with DACA was detained and deported to Mexico in a matter of days.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martin Padilla’s wife, Cynthia, relocated for several months to be closer to the border so she and the couple’s children could visit Mr. Padilla after he was deported.

item.title: Inside the Congolese Hotel Where Trump Deported 15 U.S. Migrants
item.description: They were shackled and sent to Kinshasa by the Trump administration. Now they face a dangerous choice: Go back to Latin America or stay in Africa.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean, Emiliano Rodríguez Mega, Pranav Baskar and Justin Makangara
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hugo Palencia was deported from the U.S. to Kinshasa, the Democratic Republic of Congo, last month.

item.title: Trump-Ordered Citizenship Lists for Voting Are Likely Unreliable, Justice Dept. Says
item.description: At a court hearing over a presidential order seeking to exert more control over elections, a government lawyer said no “responsible state” should rely on the lists to update their voter rolls.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Robert Nickelsberg for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s executive order would require the U.S. Postal Service to regulate mail-in voting.

item.title: Inside the Secret Mission to Fly Taiwan’s President to Africa
item.description: From satellite phone check-ins to a borrowed royal plane, new details show how Taiwan’s leader’s team outwitted China and pulled off an audacious journey to southern Africa.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: Taiwan Presidential Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A photograph provided by the Taiwan Presidential Office showed the Taiwanese leader, Lai Ching-te, waving upon arriving in Eswatini in May.

item.title: How a Drug Cartel Made a Mexican State Its Tool
item.description: Cartel insiders say that in exchange for bribes and political support they were allowed to operate in Sinaloa state with near total freedom.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cartel gunsmiths in a village on the outskirts of Culiacán, in Mexico’s Sinaloa state, in October.

item.title: The Favorites for Eurovision 2026: Finland, Australia, Greece and Others
item.description: Finland is the favorite for prediction markets and bookmakers, but singers from Australia, Denmark, Greece and Israel are coming for the title, too.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press

item.title: How to Watch the Eurovision Song Contest Final
item.description: The internationally popular event will be available to U.S. viewers, too, although not on domestic TV in some countries that are boycotting this year.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: Watching the Eurovision Song Contest at the press center in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, in 2021. The country is boycotting this year’s event.

item.title: The Damage of ‘Trump Math’ Is Adding Up
item.description: Authoritarians go after data. The president has already started.
item.dc:creator: Binyamin Appelbaum and James Robinson

item.title: Stop Looking for an ‘Offramp' in Iran. There’s No Such Thing.
item.description: And no, an exit strategy won’t help you, either.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Lozada
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Evan Hume for The New York Times

item.title: At the Summit
item.description: We wrap up President Trump’s visit to China.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Pokes Fun at Trump in China
item.description: Jimmy Fallon joked that President Trump’s idea of a Temple of Heaven is very different from the one he toured in Beijing with China’s leader.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: 5 Key Moments During the California Governor Debate
item.description: Candidates have become increasingly combative as they enter the final stretch before the June 2 primary. Once again, Xavier Becerra, a Democratic front-runner, drew most of the attacks.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall and Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Carlos Barria/Reuters
item.media:description: Seven leading candidates for California governor took part in a debate in San Francisco on Thursday.

item.title: Omaha Bar Is Not a Barbershop, State Barber Board Tells a Bar Named ‘Barber Shop’
item.description: The establishment changed its name this week from the Barber Shop Blackstone to the Censored Shop Blackstone in protest while it is suing the state in federal court.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: via Institute of Justice
item.media:description: Ms. Oltmans and Mr. DiGiacomo in the bar.

item.title: A Long Island Rail Road Strike May Be Near. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: America’s busiest passenger rail service will shut down on Saturday if workers and transit officials cannot agree on a new contract.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen
item.category: Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and Trainmen
International Assn of Machinists and Aerospace Workers
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: There has not been a strike on the Long Island Rail Road since 1994.
